stimulus: “The River-Path” is an 1857 poem by John Greenleaf Whittier. The poem conveys the speaker’s contented feelings about following an unhurried route along the water: ______blank
stem: Which quotation from the poem most effectively illustrates the claim?

answerOptions.content: “No bird-song floated down the hill, / The tangled bank below was still; / No rustle from the birchen stem, / No ripple from the water’s hem.”
“We will not walk the trodden way / Where weary feet of men delay, / Nor seek the chamber dim and small / Where lamp-light writes upon the wall.”
“I am content to wander on / Where the slow river takes the dawn, / Past mossy stone and rushy ford, / Glad of the path my heart hath stored.”
“To-day, I shall not seek the height; / Its empty wind unsettles me; / Strange shapes upon the upland white / Are not the friends I came to see.”

rationale: Choice C is the best answer because the quotation shows the speaker happily wandering along the river (“I am content to wander on / Where the slow river takes the dawn”) past natural features beside the water (“mossy stone and rushy ford”), directly illustrating the contented feelings about an unhurried river route. Choice A is incorrect because it merely describes the stillness of the scene rather than the speaker’s contentment with the route. Choice B is incorrect because the speaker describes places he will not walk, not the contented experience of the river path itself. Choice D is incorrect because the speaker describes avoiding the highlands, not enjoying a river route.
